description: Figure 11 compares impact fracture morphologies of Fe–28Mn–10Al–0.8C low-density steels: low-nitrogen (LN, 0.0025 wt% N) in (a) and high-nitrogen (HN, 0.1326 wt% N) in (b), both processed by vacuum induction melting and multidirectional hot forging. Each row comprises a 3D fracture surface height map (color scale in mm), a low-magnification SEM overview (scale bar 1 mm), and a high-magnification SEM micrograph of the rupture surface (scale bar 10 μm). The LN steel profile peaks at 7.53 mm; its low-magnification SEM shows a standard ductile impact fracture with fibrous, radial, and shear-lip zones, while its high-magnification SEM reveals elongated parabolic dimples aligned along the crack propagation direction, indicating shear-assisted fast crack growth. The HN steel profile peaks at 9.95 mm; its low-magnification SEM exhibits a more pronounced shear-lip zone, and its high-magnification SEM displays larger, deeper, and more equiaxed dimples, signifying greater plastic deformation and higher energy absorption during impact. These observations support that nitrogen addition refines austenite grains, reduces grain-boundary ferrite, and markedly improves impact toughness by hindering crack propagation.
